zach_dinkel.name: Zach Dinkel
zach_dinkel.summary: Hailing from Illinois, Zach Dinkel is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Zach's.
zach_dinkel.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zach Dinkel, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Zach is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 Midwest CRC, and so far, the only thing Zach has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Zach saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Zach is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Zach's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Zach is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Zach is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Zach wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Zach, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

zachary_wang.name: Zachary Wang
zachary_wang.summary:
Hailing from California, Zachary Wang has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 45 entries across 17 tournaments, Zachary is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Zachary is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Zachary has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 15 different players across the career:
  • Eddie Sun (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (8 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Shivani Ramakrishnan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Andy Korlann (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Zachary has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Zachary has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Zachary brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
zachary_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zachary Wang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Zachary has 45 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Zachary saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Zachary would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Zachary has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Zachary has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Zachary's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zachary plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 45 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Zachary has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Zachary's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Eddie Sun: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Eddie Sun has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Joanna Deng has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Zachary has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Zachary is not just losing locally — Zachary is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Zachary can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zachary has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zachary showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Zachary traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Zachary entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Zachary paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 45 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Zachary. Zachary just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Zachary has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Zachary. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Zachary: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

zack_nguyen.name: Zack Nguyen
zack_nguyen.summary:
A California competitor, Zack Nguyen is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U13 level, capturing 2 titles across 11 tournaments and 28 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Zack keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Zack is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Zack adapts to anyone:
  • Joanna Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (7 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Keaton Ou (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Reo Qian Bao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Jonathan Alexander surya Putra (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4)

Zack has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Zack has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Zack's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
zack_nguyen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zack Nguyen, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 wins across 28 entries in 11 tournaments. That is a 7% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Zack shows up to a tournament, Zack pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Zack saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Zack's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zack plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Zack is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Zack and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Zack needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Zack has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Joanna Zhang: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Joanna Zhang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Jonathan Alexander surya Putra has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Zack's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Zack is not just losing locally — Zack is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Nguyen family travels cross-country so Zack can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zack has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zack showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U11, which means Zack traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Zack entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Zack paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 28 entries, and 26 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Zack looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Zack's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Zack has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Zack. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

  • Shaoyao Luo (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Kimaya Krishna (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Zi Yang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Zaph has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Zaph has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Zaph consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Zaph is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
zaph_j_din_bayona.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zaph J-Din Bayona, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 62 career entries in 22 tournaments, Zaph has 5 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Zaph drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 62 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Zaph saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 South CRC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Zaph's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zaph plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 4 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Zaph can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Zaph has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Zaph's texts. The go-to partner is Arjun Aditya Hariharan (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Renee Liu has emotionally recovered.

Of Zaph's 5 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Zaph feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Zaph entered 7 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Zaph's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Zaph and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Zaph 9 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Zaph of is playing it safe. Zaph has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Zaph has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 4 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Zaph has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Zaph is not just losing locally — Zaph is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Bayona family travels cross-country so Zaph can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zaph has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zaph showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15, which means Zaph traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Zaph entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Zaph paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 62 entries, and 57 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Zaph looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Zaph's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Zaph has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Zaph. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Zelenia experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Zelenia has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Zelenia does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Zelenia has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 4 wins means Zelenia is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zelenia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Zelenia is 0-for-20. Zero titles in 20 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Zelenia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 4 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Zelenia needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Zelenia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Zelenia's texts. The most successful partnership is with Grace Cheng — 11 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Grace Cheng deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Grace Cheng a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Kevin Xie has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Zelenia entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Zelenia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Zelenia and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Zelenia 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Zelenia has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Zelenia is not just losing locally — Zelenia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shen family travels cross-country so Zelenia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zelenia has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zelenia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13, which means Zelenia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Zelenia entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Zelenia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 62 entries, and 58 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Zelenia looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Zelenia's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Zelenia has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Zelenia. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

zimeng_zhou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zimeng Zhou, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 95 career entries in 33 tournaments, Zimeng has 4 titles — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 22 entries where Zimeng drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 95 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Zimeng saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2023 NW OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Zimeng's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Zimeng actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Zimeng is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Zimeng experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Zimeng has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Zimeng does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Zimeng has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 4 wins means Zimeng is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zimeng plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Zimeng can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Zimeng has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Zimeng's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang: 16 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 16 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GD U17 — we hope Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Of Zimeng's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Zimeng feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Zimeng entered 6 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Zimeng's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Zimeng and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Zimeng has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Zimeng is not just losing locally — Zimeng is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhou family travels cross-country so Zimeng can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zimeng has 40 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zimeng showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, which means Zimeng traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Zimeng entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Zimeng paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 95 entries, and 91 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Zimeng looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Zimeng's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Zimeng has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Zimeng. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Ziyan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

  • Annie Meng — 3 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ziyan has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ziyan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 3 titles, Ziyan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
ziyan_zhao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ziyan Zhao, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ziyan has 53 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ziyan saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 NW ORC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ziyan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 12 entries — a stretch where Ziyan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ziyan is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ziyan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ziyan has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SoCal Arena CRC, where Ziyan entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U15, XD U15, GD U15). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ziyan does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Ziyan has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 3 wins means Ziyan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ziyan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ziyan is 0-for-17. Zero titles in 17 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ziyan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ziyan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 27 of them, to be precise. Ziyan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ziyan's texts. The go-to partner is Willie WANLI Yang (3 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15 — we hope Tsun Wang Lam has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Ziyan has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

One thing nobody can accuse Ziyan of is playing it safe. Ziyan has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Ziyan has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Ziyan has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ziyan is not just losing locally — Ziyan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhao family travels cross-country so Ziyan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ziyan has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ziyan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Ziyan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Ziyan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Ziyan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 53 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ziyan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ziyan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ziyan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ziyan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

zoe_wong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zoe Wong, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 15 career entries in 6 tournaments, Zoe has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Zoe drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 15 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Zoe saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zoe plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Zoe has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Tara Karthik (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Enming Cao has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Zoe has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Zoe has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. zoey_enqi_tan.name: Zoey Enqi Tan
zoey_enqi_tan.summary:
Out of California, Zoey Enqi Tan has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U19 GD, U15 GD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 12 career titles across 21 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Zoey is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (7 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U19 GD, U15 GD) — selected to represent the USA • 7 ORC titles.

Zoey has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Cassidy Yeh (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (16 total events in GD/U15/U19, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Youshi Chen — a trusted U19/XD partner (4 events, best: #5)
  • Chloe A Kai — 2 events, 1 title together in GD/U15

Zoey is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 2 titles across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Zoey is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Zoey has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Zoey has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Zoey consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 12 titles, Zoey has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
zoey_enqi_tan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zoey Enqi Tan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Zoey is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Zoey has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Zoey would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Zoey, we kept reading — all 54 entries across 21 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 22% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Zoey held up a trophy, there were roughly 3 other times Zoey walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Zoey saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Zoey burst onto the scene with a title in XD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Zoey has delivered on some of those promises: 12 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Zoey promised and what Zoey has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 11 entries — a stretch where Zoey actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 5 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Zoey experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Zoey has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Zoey does own 12 actual titles, so it is not like Zoey has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 12 wins means Zoey is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zoey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 5 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Zoey can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Zoey has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Zoey's texts. The most successful partnership is with Cassidy Yeh — 16 events, 6 titles — and honestly, Cassidy Yeh deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Cassidy Yeh a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U19 — we hope Caden Wang has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Zoey of is playing it safe. Zoey has entered older age groups at 7 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Zoey has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 10 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Zoey has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Zoey is not just losing locally — Zoey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 22% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tan family travels cross-country so Zoey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zoey has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zoey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U17, which means Zoey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U15 XD. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Zoey entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Zoey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 12 titles to the name, 54 entries, and 42 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Zoey looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Zoey's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Zoey has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Zoey. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The Zong saga began at 2022 NW Oregon OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zong has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zong showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U13, which means Zong traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NW Oregon OLC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NW ORC, where Zong entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Zong paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Zong keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Zong has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Zong, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Zong. We will be here, taking notes.

zuhao_fan.name: Zuhao Fan
zuhao_fan.summary:
Out of California, Zuhao Fan has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Zuhao's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Zuhao especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-3 finisher.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Zuhao adapts to anyone:
  • Felicia Ho (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Renee Leung (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Anthony Cheng Xu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

The 2022-2023 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Zuhao is still ascending.

Zuhao has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Zuhao has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Zuhao's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
zuhao_fan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Zuhao Fan, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 30 entries in 10 tournaments. That is a 3% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Zuhao shows up to a tournament, Zuhao pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Zuhao saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Zuhao's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Zuhao would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Zuhao has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Zuhao has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Zuhao's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Zuhao plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Zuhao is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Zuhao and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Zuhao needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Zuhao has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Zuhao's texts. The go-to partner is Felicia Ho (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Daniel Xie has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Zuhao's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Zuhao is not just losing locally — Zuhao is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Fan family travels cross-country so Zuhao can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Zuhao has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Zuhao showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U11, which means Zuhao traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Zuhao entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Zuhao paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 30 entries, and 29 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Zuhao looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Zuhao's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Zuhao has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Zuhao. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
